raw_case:
Radiological imaging of multisystem Langerhans cell histiocytosis in a young child: A rare case report
Langerhans cell histiocytosis (LCH) is a rare clonal disorder of the monocyte-macrophage system. There are 3 distinct types of LCH: multisystem type (MS), single-system multi-site (SS-m), and single-system single-site (SS-s). The disease is most common in young children, especially males. In this case, we report the rare case of a young Asian girl who initially complained of dyspnea, diarrhea, rash, and fever. Upon further examination, multiple systems were implicated, including the bone, skin, lung, liver, and spleen. Multisystem LCH with risk organ involvement give poor prognosis to the patients.

Introduction

A rare disorder of the monocyte-macrophage system, Langerhans cell histiocytosis (LCH) is characterized by the uncontrolled growth and accumulation of dendritic cells (DCs) as a result of ongoing immunological activation. Numerous clinical disorders are caused by this disease, most of which are limited to children but can also impact adults. Depending on the type of histiocyte involved, the World Health Organization (WHO) divides disorders into 3 categories: malignant histiocyte disorders, dendritic cell disorders, and disorders associated with macrophages. Among dendritic cell diseases, LCH is the most prevalent. Although it can be diagnosed in any age range, children 1-3 years old are more likely to have it. The disease is most common in young children, especially males. There are 3 to 5 cases per million children. In children, higher incidence has been reported for Hispanics [[1], [2], [3], [4], [5]].LCH is classified into 3 different types: single-system single site, single-system multi-site, and multi-system type. There are 2 types of multisystem LCH: organ involvement and noninvolvement. The skin, bones, lymph nodes, and pituitary gland are low-risk organs; the lungs, bone marrow, liver, and spleen are high-risk organs. The prognosis, course, therapy, and progress of LCH can all be impacted by the particular organ involved [6].This case involves a young Asian girl who has multisystem LCH. The role of multimodal imaging in early organ identification and disease diagnosis is emphasized.

Case presentation

A 17-month-old girl was admitted to the emergency room with complaints of shortness of breath for 12 hours before being admitted. She also had a fever, diarrhea 5 times a day, and a cough since 1 day before. The patient has failed to gain weight in the last 5 months. The patient complained of recurrent stomatitis every month since the age of 7 months. The patient also had rounded soft tissue swelling in the right temporal area measuring 3 cm in diameter that was progressively increasing for 3 months (Fig. 1). For 3 months, she had yellow skin and eyes, a swollen belly, and red rashes on her tummy, abdomen, and feet (Fig. 2).In the previous hospital 3 months ago, the patient was misdiagnosed with pulmonary tuberculosis (TB) based only chest radiograph examination, even though the Mantoux test result was negative. The patient was only given TB medication for 2 weeks. When the patient arrived at our hospital, all tests that pointed to TB were negative.The child was born by normal spontaneous vaginal delivery at term gestation, assisted by a midwife, with a birth weight of 3100 grams. He was born immediately breathing. The patient was only given the Hep-B0 vaccination and has never been given any other vaccinations to date. The baby can sit but not stand or walk yet. It can babble "mah, papa, aa," though not very clearly.Because of many signs and symptoms, the patient gets further radiological examinations, such as chest and skull radiographs, abdominal ultrasounds, chest and abdominal computed tomography (CT) scans, and head magnetic resonance imaging (MRI).In the skull, plain radiography showed osteolytic lesions of the skull vault in the temporal bone (Fig. 3). A head MRI showed a mass in the right temporal area that destroys the right temporal bone with subgaleal and intracranial infiltration, causing mass effect compression of the surrounding sulci and gyri (Fig. 4). The pituitary, basal ganglia, cerebellum, and brainstem did not show any abnormalities.A chest radiograph revealed diffuse reticulonodularity in both lungs (Fig. 5). A CT scan of the chest revealed diffuse and symmetrical reticulonodular consolidation with air bronchograms in almost all segments of the bilateral lung lobes and mediastinal lymph node enlargement (Fig. 6).Abdominal examination by ultrasound revealed inhomogeneity liver, splenomegaly and right kidney enlargement with pelvocalyectasis and dilatation of the right ureter (Fig. 7). Abdominal CT scan confirmed ultrasound findings, showing splenomegaly with parenchymal inhomogeneity and multiple hypodense lesion, enlarged caudate lobe liver with hypodense area in liver parenchyme along portal vein and biliary tract, ascites, and an enlarged right kidney, accompanied by pelvocalyectasis of the right kidney due to suspected ureteropelvic junction obstruction (UPJO) (Fig. 8).Pathologic evaluation was performed by biopsying the area in the left trunk. On macroscopic examination, a skinned tissue appears brownish-white and rubbery, measuring 0.3 × 0.3 × 0.2 cm. Microscopic examination showed that the tissue was lined with stratified squamous epithelium, and the core was normal. Subepithelially, the fibrocollagen connective tissue stroma appears in contact with inflammatory lymphocyte cells, accompanied by dilatation of blood vessels. In the dermis papillae, round-oval cells are visible that are distributed diffusely. The nuclei are reniform and eccentric, and the cytoplasm is abundant and eosinophilic. It appears that the skin adnexa consist of hair follicles, sudoriferous glands, and sebaceous glands within normal limits. The subcutis consists of mature fat cells; the nuclei are edged within normal limits (Fig. 9). The findings of the immunohistochemical tests for S100 and CD1A were positive (Fig. 10). LCH diagnosis is confirmed by immunological markers and histopathological examination.Patient has received treatment based on protocol in multisystem LCH case such as cytosine-arabinoside, vinblastine, and prednisolone. This patient has a poor prognosis because have multisystem risk organ involvement such as liver and spleen.

Discussion

The single-system LCH affects about 65%-70% of patients, while the multisystem is seen in 10% of LCH cases and has a poor prognosis that frequently results in death. Typically, a diagnosis is made during the first 2 years of life. Multisystem LCH affecting 2 or more systems or organs [4].The majority of bone involvement (75%) is unifocal and manifests as a soft tissue mass that may enlarge or pain. The most frequently affected site is the skull. LCH manifests radiographically as a single or many osteolytic lesions, sometimes with periosteal reaction [6,7]. After the bone, the skin is the organ system that is most often affected. Cutaneous illness is the most prevalent presentation in patients under 2 years old. About 87% to 93% of patients with cutaneous manifestations also have multisystem involvement. The most typical sign of multisystem LCH is cutaneous lesions accompanied by fever, hepatomegaly, splenomegaly, bone damage, and lung damage [6,7]. In multisystem disease, pulmonary involvement is common and usually manifests as tachypnea, coughing, and chest pain. The most frequent radiological abnormalities on a chest CT scan are bilateral interstitial infiltrates with reticulonodular patterns, frequently accompanied by thickening of the peribronchi [7,8]. US of the liver show hypoechoic and abdominal CT or MR images show low signal intensity along the portal veins or biliary tracts. Hypersplenism may accompany splenomegaly [6,7]. Gastrointestinal (GI) tract involvement by LCH is a rare condition and typically noted in male patients with systemic disease and is associated with both poor prognosis and high morbidity [2,9]. However, our patient had abdominal symptoms such as diarrhea and the results of the abdominal CT scan showed thickening of the ileum wall and enhancing after post injection contrast.In this case, the patient experiences various nonspecific symptoms and organ involvement making it very difficult to early diagnosis. In addition to clinical and radiological features, the diagnosis of LCH must always be predicated on the histological and immunophenotypic assessment of lesional tissue, which must be obtained from the lesion that is easiest to reach, like a skin lesion [7,10].

Conclusion

In our rare case of multisystem LCH, the patient presented with nonspecific complaints such as shortness of breath, cough, fever, diarrhea, lump on the head, history of recurrent mouth ulcers, jaundice of the skin and eyes, an enlarged abdomen and red rashes on the back, abdomen and legs, making it very difficult to establish an initial diagnosis. This case requires many approaches and further investigations such as further anamnesis regarding other sign and symptoms, and other radiological examinations, until finally signs are found that can point to a specific disease. A skin biopsy confirmed LCH. The liver and spleen are risk organ in cases of multisystem LCH and also GI tract involvement in young girl give a poor prognosis to the patients. Diagnostic imaging techniques such as chest and skull radiography, CT scans, and MRIs, radiology plays a critical role in early diagnosis and identifies the affected organ in our rare cases.

Patient consent

I confirm that written informed consent for the publication of this case report has been obtained from the patient.

generate_case.case_summary:
- **Patient Information**: 17-month-old Asian girl.
- **Chief Complaint**: Shortness of breath for 12 hours.
- **History of Present Illness**: Fever, diarrhea (5 times per day), and cough for one day. Failure to gain weight over 5 months. Recurrent stomatitis (monthly since 7 months of age). Progressive swelling of the right temporal area over 3 months, jaundice with yellowed skin and eyes, abdominal swelling, and red rashes on the back, abdomen, and feet over 3 months.
- **Past Medical History**: Misdiagnosis of pulmonary tuberculosis three months prior (based on chest radiograph; negative Mantoux test). Treated with TB medication for 2 weeks without improvement.
- **Personal History**: Born at full-term via vaginal delivery; birth weight 3100 grams. Developmentally delayed (can sit but not stand/walk). Received only Hep-B0 vaccination.
- **Family History**: None provided.
- **Physical Examination**: Rounded soft tissue swelling (3 cm in diameter) in the right temporal area. Yellowed skin and eyes, swollen abdomen, and red rashes on back, abdomen, and feet.
- **Ancillary Tests**:
  - **Skull imaging**: Osteolytic lesions in the right temporal bone with intracranial infiltration (MRI and X-ray).
  - **Chest imaging**: Diffuse reticulonodularity in both lungs (X-ray and CT scan).
  - **Abdominal imaging**: Liver inhomogeneity, splenomegaly, ascites, hypodense lesions in liver/spleen, and kidney abnormalities indicating ureteropelvic junction obstruction (ultrasound and CT scan).
  - **Histology and immunohistochemistry**: Biopsy of a skin lesion showed characteristic Langerhans cells with reniform nuclei; immunohistochemical markers S100 and CD1A were positive, confirming the diagnosis of Langerhans cell histiocytosis (LCH).
generate_case.differential_diagnosis:
- **Initial Considerations**: The patient’s nonspecific symptoms (e.g., fever, rash, cough, diarrhea, weight stagnation, jaundice, hepatosplenomegaly) prompted evaluation for systemic diseases. Differential diagnoses included malignancies (e.g., leukemia, lymphoma), infectious diseases (e.g., tuberculosis, viral hepatitis, or sepsis), metabolic disorders, and histiocytic diseases (e.g., Langerhans cell histiocytosis).
- **Systematic Analysis**:
  - **Malignancy (e.g., leukemia/lymphoma)**: Excluded due to lack of bone marrow involvement or mediastinal mass; imaging findings and biopsy favored histiocytic disease.
  - **Infectious causes (e.g., tuberculosis, sepsis)**: Excluded based on negative infection tests and failure of antituberculosis treatment; lung findings were atypical for TB or pneumonia.
  - **Metabolic/genetic conditions**: Excluded based on imaging findings consistent with osteolytic lesions and systemic involvement of high-risk organs.
  - **Histiocytic disorders (e.g., Langerhans cell histiocytosis)**: Considered due to hallmark findings of osteolytic bone lesions, skin rash, hepatosplenomegaly, and morphologic features on biopsy (reniform nuclei of Langerhans cells). Confirmation with positive S100 and CD1A markers established the diagnosis.
generate_case.final_diagnosis: Multisystem Langerhans Cell Histiocytosis (MS-LCH) with risk organ involvement (liver and spleen).
generate_case.diagnosis_results: Multisystem Langerhans Cell Histiocytosis (MS-LCH) with risk organ involvement.

results.thinking_process:
Okay, let's tackle this case. The patient is a 17-month-old girl with a bunch of symptoms. First, her chief complaint is shortness of breath for 12 hours. But looking at the history, she's had fever, diarrhea, and cough for a day. Then there's failure to gain weight over five months. Recurrent stomatitis every month since she was 7 months old. Then, over three months, she's had swelling in the right temporal area, jaundice, abdominal swelling, and red rashes. 

Hmm, let's start breaking this down. The recurrent stomatitis stands out. Monthly mouth sores starting at 7 months. That could be due to immune issues, maybe. Then the right temporal swelling. The imaging showed osteolytic lesions there with intracranial infiltration. That makes me think of something infiltrating the bone. Maybe a tumor or granulomatous disease. But she was misdiagnosed with TB three months ago based on chest X-ray, but Mantoux was negative. TB treatment didn't help. So maybe it's not TB.

The chest imaging shows diffuse reticulonodularity. In kids, that can be from infections, interstitial lung diseases, or maybe something like LCH. Her abdominal imaging shows liver issues, splenomegaly, ascites, and lesions in liver/spleen. Also kidney problems. The skin biopsy came back with Langerhans cells positive for CD1a and S100. Wait, the histology already confirms Langerhans cell histiocytosis (LCH). Oh, right, the ancillary tests include that. So the diagnosis is confirmed. But why is there jaundice and liver issues?

LCH can affect multiple systems. The liver involvement might explain the jaundice and ascites. The skin rashes could be part of LCH's manifestations. The temporal bone lesion is a classic site for LCH. The lung involvement with reticulonodular pattern is also consistent. Recurrent stomatitis might be related to immune dysfunction from LCH or secondary infections. The failure to thrive could be due to chronic illness and malabsorption from diarrhea. 

But wait, the histology already confirmed LCH via skin biopsy. So even though there was a previous misdiagnosis of TB, the definitive test here is positive for LCH. The combination of multisystem involvement (bone, skin, lungs, liver, spleen, possibly kidneys) fits multisystem LCH. The presence of CD1a positive cells is diagnostic. So the answer is Langerhans cell histiocytosis.
results.content:
### Resoning:
<step 1> The patient presents with multisystem involvement, including osteolytic skull lesions, diffuse lung reticulonodularity, hepatosplenomegaly, ascites, skin rashes, recurrent stomatitis, and developmental delay. These findings suggest a systemic disorder rather than an isolated condition.  
<step 2> The temporal bone lesion with intracranial infiltration and osteolytic features raises suspicion for Langerhans cell histiocytosis (LCH), which commonly involves the skull and causes lytic lesions. The positive immunohistochemistry (S100, CD1A) from the skin biopsy confirms the presence of Langerhans cells, pathognomonic for LCH.  
<step 3> The pulmonary reticulonodular pattern, hepatic/splenic hypodense lesions, and skin rashes align with multisystem LCH. Jaundice and ascites likely reflect liver dysfunction due to LCH infiltration or secondary biliary obstruction.  
<step 4> Recurrent stomatitis and failure to thrive further support chronic systemic inflammation or immune dysregulation associated with LCH. The misdiagnosis of tuberculosis (despite negative Mantoux test) highlights the challenge of distinguishing LCH radiographically from granulomatous diseases.  
<step 5> The absence of response to TB treatment and the conclusive histopathological evidence of LCH confirm the diagnosis, ruling out infections, malignancies, or metabolic disorders.  

### Answer:
Langerhans cell histiocytosis (LCH)

reasoning_eval.step: The patient presents with multisystem involvement, including osteolytic skull lesions, diffuse lung reticulonodularity, hepatosplenomegaly, ascites, skin rashes, recurrent stomatitis, and developmental delay. These findings suggest a systemic disorder rather than an isolated condition.

The temporal bone lesion with intracranial infiltration and osteolytic features raises suspicion for Langerhans cell histiocytosis (LCH), which commonly involves the skull and causes lytic lesions. The positive immunohistochemistry (S100, CD1A) from the skin biopsy confirms the presence of Langerhans cells, pathognomonic for LCH.

The pulmonary reticulonodular pattern, hepatic/splenic hypodense lesions, and skin rashes align with multisystem LCH. Jaundice and ascites likely reflect liver dysfunction due to LCH infiltration or secondary biliary obstruction.

Recurrent stomatitis and failure to thrive further support chronic systemic inflammation or immune dysregulation associated with LCH. The misdiagnosis of tuberculosis (despite negative Mantoux test) highlights the challenge of distinguishing LCH radiographically from granulomatous diseases.
The absence of response to TB treatment and the conclusive histopathological evidence of LCH confirm the diagnosis, ruling out infections, malignancies, or metabolic disorders.

gt_reasoning_eval.step: Initial symptoms prompted evaluation for systemic diseases including malignancies, infectious diseases, metabolic disorders, and histiocytic diseases.
Malignancy (e.g., leukemia/lymphoma) was excluded due to lack of bone marrow involvement or mediastinal mass; imaging and biopsy favored histiocytic disease.
Infectious causes (e.g., tuberculosis, sepsis) were excluded based on negative infection tests and atypical lung findings.
Metabolic/genetic conditions were excluded based on imaging findings consistent with osteolytic lesions and systemic involvement of high-risk organs.

Histiocytic disorders (e.g., Langerhans cell histiocytosis) were considered due to osteolytic bone lesions, skin rash, hepatosplenomegaly, and morphologic features on biopsy (reniform nuclei of Langerhans cells).
Diagnosis confirmed with positive S100 and CD1A markers.
Final diagnosis: Multisystem Langerhans Cell Histiocytosis (MS-LCH) with risk organ involvement (liver and spleen).
